data.heading: Organisation
data.content: An organisation represents a building, property, or community set up inside ProPorto. Each organisation manages its own users, workflows, communication, and records independently. If you belong to more than one organisation, your role and permissions may be different in each one.

data.heading: Users
data.content: Users are the people who belong to an organisation. A user can have different roles in different organisations. An Admin has full access to manage everything. A User can view and manage most items. A Viewer has limited access and can only see certain information.

data.heading: Groups
data.content: Groups allow an organisation to organise users into teams or committees. Groups make it easier to send announcements, create meetings, assign tasks, or share updates with a specific set of people.

data.heading: Workflow
data.content: A workflow is a structured process created to manage an issue or task from start to finish—for example, repairing a water tank or upgrading lighting. A workflow can contain tasks, quotes, invoices, and evidences along with their own attachments and images that help track the entire journey from problem to completion.

data.heading: Workflow Categories
data.content: We can create our own workflow categories. Categories help classify workflows into predefined types, such as 'Complaints', 'Upgrades', 'Maintenance', or 'Safety'. They make it easier to organise and search for workflows.

data.heading: Vendors
data.content: Vendors are external service providers, such as plumbers, electricians, cleaners, or maintenance companies. You can send them quotes, receive invoices, and keep track of their work within ProPorto.

data.heading: Tasks
data.content: Tasks are specific actions or steps within a workflow. For example: ‘Get a quotation’, ‘Schedule repair’, or ‘Approve invoice’. Tasks can be assigned to different users to ensure the workflow progresses smoothly.

data.heading: Quotes
data.content: Quotes are price estimates received from vendors. You can request multiple quotes for a workflow, compare them, and choose the best option. All quotes stay linked to the workflow for easy reference.

data.heading: Work Orders
data.content: Work orders are approved jobs assigned to vendors to carry out the actual work. Once a quote is accepted, a work order can be created to track progress, schedules, costs, and completion — all linked back to the workflow for full visibility.

data.heading: Invoices
data.content: Invoices are bills received from vendors after work is completed. They are stored separately from quotes and help keep financial records organised. Invoices can also be used for approval and audit purposes inside the workflow.

data.heading: Evidences
data.content: Evidences are photos, documents, or notes attached to show the condition before and after work is done. For example, you can upload an image showing damage and another showing the completed repair. Evidences help verify progress and maintain transparency.

data.heading: Financial Year
data.content: A Financial Year defines the accounting period for an organisation, typically spanning 12 months. It helps group all financial activities such as budgets, transactions, and reports within a specific timeframe, making it easier to track performance and ensure compliance.

data.heading: Chart of Accounts
data.content: The Chart of Accounts is a structured list of all financial accounts used by an organisation, such as assets, liabilities, income, and expenses. It provides a clear framework to categorise every financial transaction, ensuring consistency, accuracy, and proper reporting.

data.heading: Budget
data.content: A Budget outlines the planned income and expenses for a financial year. It helps organisations allocate funds, control spending, and make informed financial decisions. Budgets can be compared against actual transactions to monitor performance and identify variances.

data.heading: Ledger
data.content: The Ledger is the central record of all financial transactions, organised by accounts from the Chart of Accounts. It provides a detailed and chronological view of how money moves through the organisation, forming the foundation for financial reporting and audits.

data.heading: Transactions
data.content: Transactions represent individual financial activities such as payments, receipts, or transfers. Each transaction is recorded with details like date, amount, and associated accounts, ensuring every financial movement is traceable and properly accounted for.

data.heading: Announcements
data.content: Announcements allow admins or authorised users to broadcast important messages to all users or to selected groups. These are useful for updates like water shutdown notices, maintenance schedules, or community alerts.

data.heading: Meetings
data.content: Meetings provide a way to schedule and organise gatherings for the organisation or specific groups. Details such as time, date, agenda, and participants can be shared easily with everyone involved.

data.heading: Votes
data.content: Votes allow the organisation to collect opinions or make decisions as a group. Users can choose one option from a set of choices. Results can help the committee or management understand the community's preference.

data.heading: User Requests
data.content: User Requests allow residents or members to submit personal requests, such as access requests, complaints, or service needs. These requests can be reviewed, approved, or acted on by the management.

data.heading: Booking
data.content: The Booking module allows users to reserve shared facilities such as the gym, lift, BBQ area, or meeting rooms. Bookings help avoid conflicts and ensure fair usage of common facilities.

data.heading: Booking Types
data.content: Booking Types define what can be booked inside an organisation—for example, the lift, gym, conference room, or tennis court. Each type can have its own rules and availability.

data.heading: Assets
data.content: Assets represent physical items or areas within a property, such as lifts, fire systems, common facilities, or equipment. Assets help you keep track of maintenance history, related work orders, documents, and overall condition — all in one place.

data.heading: Asset Types
data.content: Asset types are templates used to group similar assets together, like lifts, pumps, or fire equipment. They define common details and maintenance requirements, making it easier to manage assets consistently across properties.

data.heading: Document Store
data.content: The document store is a central place to create, upload, and organise files. Documents can be categorised into folders for easy access, helping you keep contracts, reports, certificates, and records neatly structured and easy to find.

data.heading: Other Information
data.content: Other information are data that can have the general rules or on-boarding documentation etc. that will be helpful for all users in the organisation.
